request.question: In which airport is the head office of the airline which joined with the government of Sierra Leone to form Leone Airways?

title: Tower Hill (Sierra Leone)
Patrick Omolade Hamilton
Oku people (Sierra Leone)
Government of Sierra Leone
Leone Airways
Special Court for Sierra Leone
Mohamed Sillah
Parliament of Sierra Leone
Arik Air
Sierra Leone Anti-corruption Commission
sentences: Tower Hill is an affluent government and residential neighborhood in central Freetown, Sierra Leone.
Tower Hill is located within the central business district in downtown Freetown.
Tower Hill is the seat of the government of Sierra Leone, as it is home to the State House (the office and residence of the Sierra Leone president).

Tower Hill is also home to the Sierra Leone House of Parliament, the Sierra Leone Ministry of Defence, the Bank of Sierra Leone , The Sierra Leone National Electoral Commission (NEC) , and many other government buildings.
Patrick Omolade Hamilton is a Sierra Leone judge and an associate justice in the Supreme Court of Sierra Leone.
He had previously served as a judge in the Sierra Leone High Court and the Sierra Leone Court of Appeal.
He was appointed as a Supreme Court justice by Sierra Leone's president Ernest Bai Koroma and took office on January 22, 2009 after being confirmed by the Sierra Leone Parliament.
The Oku people, also commonly known as Oku Mohammedans or "Aku Mohammedans"in Sierra Leone and as the "Aku Marabou" or "Oku Marabou" in the Gambia, are an ethnic group in Sierra Leone and the Gambia.

The Oku people are the descendants of liberated Africans of Yoruba descent from Southwest Nigeria who were liberated or came to Sierra Leone as settlers in the mid 19th century and formed a distinctive ethnic group The Oku are virtually all Muslims and are known for their conservative muslim population.
The British colonial government provided official recognition to the Oku Mohammedan community as a distinctive community in Sierra Leone.
Although the Sierra Leone government officially considered the Oku people as members of the Creole ethnic group, many Sierra Leoneans consider the Oku people as a distinctive ethnic group.
The government of Sierra Leone is the governing authority of the Republic of Sierra Leone, as established by the Sierra Leone Constitution.
The Sierra Leone government is divided into three branches: the executive, legislative and the judicial.
The seat of government of Sierra Leone is in the capital Freetown.
Leone Airways was a planned national carrier for Sierra Leone.
It was established as a joint venture between the government of Sierra Leone and Arik Airlines of Nigeria.

The Special Court for Sierra Leone, or the "Special Court" (SCSL), also called the Sierra Leone Tribunal, was a judicial body set up by the government of Sierra Leone and the United Nations to "prosecute persons who bear the greatest responsibility for serious violations of international humanitarian law and Sierra Leonean law" committed in Sierra Leone after 30 November 1996 and during the Sierra Leone Civil War.
The court's working language was English.
The court listed offices in Freetown, The Hague, and New York City.
Mohamed Yahya Sillah was born December 12, 1949 in Massam Kpaka, Pujehun District, Republic of Sierra Leone.
His father, Alhaji Yahya Sillah, now 100 years old, served as Regent Chief (Acting Paramount Chief) for several years in Dama Chiefdom, Kenema District in the early seventies.
Alhaji Yahya Sillah was appointed to that high position by Sierra Leone President Siaka Stevens.
His mother, Haja Mamie Zoe Mansaray, was a home maker.
A journalist and human rights activist, Mohamed Yahya Sillah was a vocal torch-bearer in the transition efforts from military to civilian rule in Sierra Leone, 1996.

As Leader and National Chairman of National Alliance Democratic Party (NADP), he competed the 1996 Presidential elections in Sierra Leone, becoming one of only thirteen political leaders that qualified to contest the Presidential and Parliamentary elections in the country at the age of 46.
His adoring personality lured many Sierra Leoneans and media practitioners to view him as one of the most eloquent and charismatic politicians in Sierra Leone.
Nevertheless, he received only 0.5% of the votes (3,723 total votes), good for 13th place.
Although he protested the election results on BBC's Focus on Africa, he later suspended his protest "for the sake of peace."
Mohamed Yahya Sillah continues to enjoy massive support from the youth and women supporters in Sierra Leone.
In 2007, he withdrew his bid for national leadership in the Presidential and Parliamentary elections, citing inadequate funding and ineffective campaign management machinery.
He later put his weight behind the Sierra Leone Peoples Party (SLPP) presidential candidate, Vice President Solomon Berewa.
When Berewa lost the elections, Mohamed Yahya Sillah gracefully congratulated the winning candidate, Ernest Bai Koroma, of the All People's Congress (APC) party.
Mohamed Yahya Sillah vows to respect and help protect the dignity of any Sierra Leonean that is legitimately and democratically elected to office by the people of Sierra Leone.
Parliament of Sierra Leone is the legislative branch of the government of Sierra Leone.
It is principally responsible for making laws.
The Sierra Leone parliament consists of 124 members, of which 112 members are directly elected from across Sierra Leone's 14 Districts.
The Parliament is led by the Speaker.
The current speaker of Parliament is Sheku Badara Bashiru Dumbuya (APC).
The current elected members of parliament are entirely composed of members of the All People's Congress and the Sierra Leone People's Party, the two largest political parties in Sierra Leone.
Arik Air is a Nigerian airline operating mainly from two hubs at Murtala Muhammed International Airport near Lagos and Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport in Abuja.
Arik Air's head office is the "Arik Air Aviation Center" on the grounds of Murtala Muhammed International Airport in Ikeja.
Arik Air serves a network of regional and mid-haul destinations within Africa.

The Sierra Leone Anti-corruption Commission (ACC-SL) commonly known as ACC is an independent agency of the Sierra Leone Sierra Leone Government, that investigates and prosecutes corruption cases in Sierra Leone.
The ACC is supervised by the Sierra Leone Ministry of Justice.
The current Head of the ACC is Ady Macauley, who has been in office since March 14, 2016.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: In which airport is the head office of the airline which joined with the government of Sierra Leone to form Leone Airways?
hop1_docs: Leone Airways | Leone Airways was a planned national carrier for Sierra Leone.  It was established as a joint venture between the government of Sierra Leone and Arik Airlines of Nigeria.

West African Airways Corporation | West African Airways Corporation, or WAAC for short, was an airline that operated from 1946 to 1958, jointly owned by the governments of Britain's four west African colonies, namely The Gambia, the Gold Coast (now Ghana), Nigeria, and Sierra Leone.  The carrier was headquartered at the "Airways House" in Ikeja, Nigeria, and operated from its hub in Lagos Airport.  It was dissolved on none }} , after all the shareholder countries but Nigeria set up their own national airlines following their independence.  As the sole remaining major stockholder of the airline, the government of Nigeria continued to operate it as WAAC Nigeria, which was eventually renamed Nigeria Airways and became the flag carrier of the country.

Peoples Party (Sierra Leone) | The Peoples Party of Sierra Leone was a political party formed by Lamina Sankoh in 1948.  The party sought to unite the British Crown Colony of Freetown with the protectorate of Sierra Leone, which were governed separately until independence in 1961.  The party merged with 2 other organizations (The Protectorate Education Progressive Union and The Sierra Leone Organisation Society) in 1951 to form the Sierra Leone Peoples Party (SLPP), which is today one of the two major parties in the government of the country.

President of Sierra Leone | The President of the Republic of Sierra Leone is the head of state and the head of government of Sierra Leone, as well as the commander-in-chief of the Armed Forces.  The President appoints and heads a Cabinet of ministers (including the Vice President), which must be approved by the Parliament.

Sierra National Airlines | Sierra National Airlines was the national airline of Sierra Leone.  It was based at the Lungi International Airport, in Lungi, Sierra Leone.  It operated scheduled domestic, regional and international services.
Fly 6ix | Fly 6ix is a former passenger airline with its head office in Freetown, Sierra Leone, operating out of Lungi International Airport.

Sierra Leone Anti-corruption Commission | The Sierra Leone Anti-corruption Commission (ACC-SL) commonly known as ACC is an independent agency of the Sierra Leone Sierra Leone Government, that investigates and prosecutes corruption cases in Sierra Leone.  The ACC is supervised by the Sierra Leone Ministry of Justice.  The current Head of the ACC is Ady Macauley, who has been in office since March 14, 2016.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Leone Airways — [1] — "Leone Airways was a planned national carrier for Sierra Leone."
- Arik Airlines — [1] — "Arik Airlines of Nigeria."
- Sierra National Airlines — [5] — "Sierra National Airlines was the national airline of Sierra Leone."
- Lungi International Airport — [5] — "Lungi International Airport, in Lungi, Sierra Leone."
- Fly 6ix head office — [6] — "head office in Freetown, Sierra Leone, operating out of Lungi International Airport."
- WAAC hub/headquarters — [2] — "operated from its hub in Lagos Airport." / "headquartered at the "Airways House" in Ikeja, Nigeria"

Ambiguities:
- "Arik Airlines" label may vary (passage uses "Arik Airlines"); common page title may be "Arik Air".
- "Lagos Airport" (passage [2]) could refer to different named airports (e.g., "Murtala Muhammed International Airport") — not specified here.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing fact: the specific airport in which Arik Airlines' (Arik Air) head office is located is not stated in the provided passages.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page: "Arik Air" (Wikipedia page titled exactly "Arik Air").
- Search query: "Arik Air head office airport" (include quotes).
- Search query: "Arik Airlines headquarters airport Lagos" (include quotes).
- If needed, open disambiguation: "Arik Air (disambiguation)" or search "Arik Airlines headquarters Murtala Muhammed International Airport".
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Arik Air | Arik Air is a Nigerian airline operating mainly from two hubs at Murtala Muhammed International Airport near Lagos and Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport in Abuja.  Arik Air's head office is the "Arik Air Aviation Center" on the grounds of Murtala Muhammed International Airport in Ikeja.  Arik Air serves a network of regional and mid-haul destinations within Africa.

Skyways (airline) | Skyways Express AB was a Swedish airline that operated regional and domestic scheduled services.  With its corporate head office on the property of Stockholm-Arlanda Airport in Sigtuna Municipality, Sweden, its main hub was at Stockholm-Arlanda Airport.  The airline was placed into administration on 22 May 2012.  Previously its head office was at Eurostop köpcenter, Cederströms slinga, Arlandastad.

Sosoliso Airlines | Sosoliso Airlines Limited was a scheduled, domestic, passenger airline.  For much of its existence it had its head office in Ikeja, Lagos State.  Originally its head office was on the grounds of Enugu Airport in Enugu.

Air Alps | Air Alps (Air Alps Aviation, Alpenländisches Flugunternehmen Ges.m.b.H.)  was an airline with its head office at Innsbruck Airport in Innsbruck, Austria.  It was an independent regional airline serving 10 cities in Italy from Milan and Bolzano, international services from Milan, as well as charter services.  Its main base was Innsbruck Airport, with hub at Malpensa Airport, Milan.  Formerly its head office was elsewhere in Innsbruck.

SCAT Airlines | SCAT Airlines, legally "PLL SCAT Air Company", is an airline with its head office on the property of Shymkent Airport in Shymkent, Kazakhstan.  It operates services to all major cities of Kazakhstan and to neighbouring countries.  Its main base is Shymkent Airport, with focus cities at Oral Ak Zhol Airport, Aqtau Airport, Astana International Airport, Atyrau Airport, Kyzylorda Airport and Almaty International Airport.

Portugália Airlines | Portugália Airlines is a Portuguese regional airline with its head office on the grounds of Lisbon Portela Airport in Lisbon.  It is a subsidiary of TAP Portugal and operates scheduled international and domestic services from its bases at Lisbon Airport and Porto Airport on behalf of TAP Express.

Olympic Airlines | Olympic Airlines (Greek: Ολυμπιακές Αερογραμμές , "Olympiakés Aerogrammés" – OA), formerly named Olympic Airways for at least four decades, was the flag carrier airline of Greece.  The airline's head office was located in Athens.  The airline operated services to 37 domestic destinations and to 32 destinations world-wide.  The airline's main base was at Athens International Airport, with hubs at Thessaloniki International Airport, "Macedonia", Heraklion International Airport, "Nikos Kazantzakis" and Rhodes International Airport, "Diagoras".  Olympic Airlines also owned a base at London Heathrow International Airport.  By December 2007, the airline employed about 8,500 staff.
response.summary_2: Arik Airlines' head office is on the grounds of Murtala Muhammed International Airport in Ikeja.
